name: DHCP Protocol (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)
description: Advanced quiz on dynamic IP allocation, DORA messages, UDP and encapsulation.

questions.question: In network architecture, what is the main goal of how the DHCP protocol works?
options: The protocol resolves textual website names into their respective logical cloud IPs.
The protocol converts ranges of private IPs into a routable public IP for the exact exit.
The protocol dynamically allocates IP addresses to hosts arriving on the local network.
The protocol ensures strict sequential delivery by handling frames at the link layer.

questions.question: How is a DHCP message structurally and sequentially encapsulated to travel across the local link?
options: It goes down encapsulated inside UDP, which goes inside IP, and finally into the Ethernet frame.
It goes down encapsulated in the pure TCP mesh, reaching the physical layer as photons.
It is strictly divided into reserved circuits before reaching the IP core of the network.
It travels without encapsulation because the client's address is still completely null.

questions.question: What is the exact sequence of messages exchanged in the classic IP allocation process by DHCP?
options: Offer, Discover, Request, and the exact Acknowledgement (ACK).
Request, Acknowledgement (ACK), Discover, and Offer.
Acknowledgement (ACK), Discover, Offer, and the Request.
Discover, Offer, Request, and the exact Acknowledgement (ACK).

questions.question: Besides the allocated IP address, what other vital information can DHCP send to the host?
options: The exact physical route to web servers, plus the session key for the IP traffic.
The first-hop router address, the DNS server IP and the local subnet mask.
The Wi-Fi network's WPA2 encryption key, along with the domain's local routing table.
Only and exclusively the TCP port number, because routers fill in the IP at the link.

questions.question: Why does a newly connected client send the first DHCP messages in 'Broadcast' mode?
options: Because the physical layer requires radio communication to reach all local antennas.
Because it has no IP of its own and does not know the official IP address of the DHCP server.
Because it wants to notify all hosts on the network that it will monopolize the router signal.
Because the link protocol needs to validate transmissions before delegating the port.

questions.question: What is the server's reaction when it receives a DHCP Request asking for an IP that is already in use?
options: The server retransmits a DHCPDecline, silencing the intruding client for a few minutes.
The router physically disconnects the new computer so as not to cause congestion.
The server issues a DHCPNAK requiring the client to restart the process at discovery.
The system provisionally reserves an IPv6 address to prevent the failure.

questions.question: What does the concept that the DHCP protocol works based on IP 'leases' mean?
options: It means the IP is granted for a limited time and can be renewed by the host.
It means the local provider charges a special fee for each established connection.
It means the IP address will be bound forever to that exact physical MAC card.
It means routers hold all datagrams in a buffer until that lease ends.

questions.question: Looking at the transport structure of a DHCP message, which logical ports are used?
options: The requests use web ports such as port 80 for the exit and port 443 on arrival.
The protocol demands ports above 1024 to avoid blind router blocking.
The DHCP message uses no transport port, operating exclusively at the IP link.
Communication occurs natively through the standard use of port 67 and also port 68.

questions.question: What is the exact and main purpose of the 'Transaction ID' field in the network's DHCP messages?
options: To actively store the whole amount of data transferred during the usage period.
To securely and uniquely link the server's response to the exact initial request.
To encrypt the information using a heavy protocol on fiber routers at the edges.
To precisely define the best path to be taken on the packet's journey through the ISP.

questions.question: In the classic home network scenario, where is the DHCP server usually housed?
options: It runs entirely on a remote server hosted by the ISP within the global DNS system.
It comes integrated and co-located inside the access router that serves the network.
It is actively embedded in all of the host's terminals, acting in a hidden mode.
It is virtually split across the LAN's various Ethernet hardware switches.
